title: Pedestrian behaviors at and perceptions towards various pedestrian facilities: an examination based on observation and survey data
authors: V.P. Sisiopiku; D. Akin
research_problem: Understanding pedestrian behaviors and perceptions toward various pedestrian facilities including signalized and unsignalized intersection crosswalks, unsignalized midblock crosswalks, physical barriers (vegetation and concrete walls), midblock crosswalk shelters, colored paving at medians and curbs, and pedestrian warning signs at midblock locations.
method_summary: Mixed methods approach combining: (1) observational study using video cameras to record pedestrian movements at 8 locations along a 1km divided boulevard section of Grand River Avenue in East Lansing, Michigan; (2) user survey of 711 daily and occasional pedestrians collecting information on crossing patterns, preferences, perceptions of safety, and factors influencing crossing decisions. Spatial and temporal crossing compliance rates calculated from video data and compared with survey responses.
method_family: Mixed Methods - Field Observation + Survey
tasks: Pedestrian crossing compliance analysis; Assessment of pedestrian perceptions toward pedestrian facilities; Comparison of self-reported behavior with observed behavior
datasets: Video data from 8 video cameras recording pedestrian movements (16-18 hours per session); User survey responses (n=711) from Michigan State University affiliates
metrics: Spatial Crossing Compliance Rate (SCCR) - percentage of pedestrians crossing within crosswalk area; Temporal Crossing Compliance Rate (TCCR) - percentage complying with WALK signal indication; Pedestrian Crossing Compliance Rate (PCCR) - combined spatial and temporal compliance
claim_text: Unsignalized midblock crosswalks are the most preferred pedestrian facility (83% reported preference) and demonstrate high crossing compliance (71.2%)
limitations: Sample population primarily consists of university affiliates (MSU students, staff, faculty), which may not represent general pedestrian population | Low survey response rate (17.9%) may introduce non-response bias | Study conducted at a single site (Grand River Avenue), limiting generalizability to other contexts | Data collection limited to weekdays and excluded rainy days and football weekends, potentially missing relevant behavioral variations
notes: Study site was a recently renovated 1km section of Grand River Avenue next to Michigan State University campus. The research was sponsored by Michigan Department of Transportation. Key finding: midblock crosswalks more effective in achieving design purpose compared to signalized crosswalks, though signalized crosswalks better for pedestrian channelization. Leading pedestrian intervals recommended for improving pedestrian safety at signalized intersections with significant turning volumes.

title: Modeling pedestrians' road crossing behavior in traffic system micro-simulation in China
authors: Jianguo Yang; Wen Deng; Jinmei Wang; Qingfeng Li; Zhaoan Wang
venue: Transportation Research Part A: Policy and Practice
research_problem: Pedestrian road crossing behavior in Chinese cities differs from developed countries due to high rates of signal non-compliance. Existing foreign micro-simulation tools do not work effectively in China because they cannot represent the high rate of pedestrian red light running and mixed traffic characteristics typical of Chinese cities.
method_summary: A pedestrian model for traffic system micro-simulation in China that classifies pedestrians into two types: law-obeying and opportunistic. Questionnaires (495 respondents) determined proportions of pedestrian types under different circumstances. Time gap distributions extracted from videotape (989 samples) determined the safety criterion (ΔT) for pedestrians to decide whether to walk or wait when conflicting with vehicle flows. Parameters were calibrated and adjusted to align simulation results with field observations.
method_family: Microscopic behavioral simulation model
tasks: Pedestrian road crossing behavior modeling; Traffic micro-simulation; Pedestrian violation behavior analysis
datasets: Questionnaire data from 495 respondents in Xi'an (November 22-23, 2003); Videotape data from zebra crossing outside Southeast Gate of Xi'an Jiaotong University (989 samples); Field data from crossings without police on duty (June 2003 to April 2004)
metrics: Proportion of red light jumpers; Proportion of violation followers; Time gap acceptance (ΔT); Pedestrian arriving interval distribution
claim_text: The model successfully represents the high rate of pedestrian red light running and mixed traffic characteristics in Chinese cities
limitations: The model only models pedestrians' decision making of red light running according to the instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles; it does not account for situations when oncoming vehicles change their states (accelerate, decelerate, etc.) | The calculation method of ΔT needs improvement for turning vehicles to make the model more practical for simulating Chinese city traffic | Questionnaire respondents may not represent all pedestrians, and some answers may not reflect actual behavior, leading to parameter calibration uncertainties
notes: The study was conducted in Xi'an, China; Pedestrian red light running rates in Guangzhou reached 66.7% in certain sections according to 2003 report; The Road Traffic Safety Law implemented on May 1, 2004 increased punishment for pedestrian violations; Police presence did not have distinct impact on pedestrian compliance according to questionnaire results

title: Application of social force model to pedestrian behavior analysis at signalized crosswalk
authors: Weiliang Zeng; Peng Chen; Hideki Nakamura; Miho Iryo-Asano
venue: Transportation Research Part C: Emerging Technologies
research_problem: Limited pedestrian behavior models exist for signalized crosswalks, where pedestrian behavior involves group or individual evasion with surrounding pedestrians, collision avoidance with conflicting vehicles, and response to signal control and crosswalk boundary. The study addresses this gap by developing a microscopic simulation model for pedestrian behavior analysis at signalized intersections.
method_summary: A modified social force model is developed for pedestrian behavior at signalized crosswalks, incorporating five sources of social force: driving force toward destination, repulsive/attractive force from crosswalk boundary, repulsive force from surrounding pedestrians, repulsive force from conflicting vehicles, and attractive force from signal phase. Parameters are separated into measurable (directly estimated from observed trajectory data) and non-measurable (derived by maximum likelihood estimation) groups.
method_family: Social force model (modified)
tasks: Pedestrian trajectory estimation; Stop/go decision modeling at pedestrian flashing green phase; Collision avoidance behavior with vehicles; Lane formation phenomenon reproduction; Passing position distribution validation
datasets: North crosswalk at Kanayama intersection in Nagoya City, Japan; 1352 pedestrian trajectories; 124 left-turning vehicle trajectories; 97,200 position samples
metrics: Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) for position (5.7%); Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) for speed (10.5%); Two-sample t-test for passing position distribution; Lane formation count comparison
claim_text: The modified social force model achieves 5.7% MAPE for position estimation and 10.5% MAPE for speed estimation in trajectory reproduction
limitations: The reaction of pedestrians to vehicles is more sensitive in simulation than in real world (peak shift 2.5m vs 2m observed) | Pedestrian heterogeneous characteristics such as age, gender, and partner relationship are not considered as influencing factors | Model calibrated for left-hand traffic (Japan); may not directly apply to right-hand traffic regions
notes: Study focuses on Japanese signalized intersections with left-hand traffic; Pedestrian flashing green (PFG) phase modeling is crucial for safety assessment; Model considers both repulsive and attractive effects from crosswalk boundary; Stochastic adjustment mechanism developed to avoid pedestrians stopping forever due to social force balance

notes: Study focuses on Japanese signalized intersections with left-hand traffic; Pedestrian flashing green (PFG) phase modeling is crucial for safety assessment; Model considers both repulsive and attractive effects from crosswalk boundary; Stochastic adjustment mechanism developed to avoid pedestrians stopping forever due to social force balance

research_problem: Identify potential external features on fully autonomous vehicles (FAVs) that help pedestrians understand intended vehicle behavior at crosswalks, improve receptivity toward FAVs, and influence crossing behavior. title: Modeling pedestrians' road crossing behavior in traffic system micro-simulation in China
authors: Jianguo Yang; Wen Deng; Jinmei Wang; Qingfeng Li; Zhaoan Wang
research_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of modeling pedestrian road crossing behavior in Chinese cities, where high rates of signal non-compliance (red light running) are common and differ significantly from pedestrians in developed countries. Existing micro-simulation tools developed abroad fail to accurately describe Chinese traffic situations due to unique characteristics like mixed traffic flows and widespread pedestrian violations.
method_summary: The paper proposes a pedestrian micro-simulation model that classifies pedestrians into two types: law-obeying (Type I) and opportunistic (Type II). The model uses questionnaires to determine the proportions of each pedestrian type under different circumstances (r1, r2), and extracts time gap distributions from videotape to calibrate the gap acceptance criterion (DT) for pedestrians deciding whether to walk or wait when conflicting with vehicle flows. The model framework incorporates external factors including policeman presence, other pedestrians' behavior, and vehicle flow states.
method_family: Microscopic simulation / Agent-based modeling
tasks: Pedestrian red light running behavior modeling; Gap acceptance decision modeling for pedestrian crossing; Simulation of pedestrian-vehicle interactions at signalized intersections
datasets: Questionnaire data from 495 respondents in Xi'an (200 students from Xi'an Jiaotong University and 300 random pedestrians); Videotape data from zebra crossing outside Southeast Gate of Xi'an Jiaotong University (989 samples extracted for DT calculations)
metrics: Proportion of red light jumpers; Proportion of violation followers (pedestrians who follow others to violate); Comparison of pedestrian arriving interval distributions
claim_text: The proposed model successfully represents the high rate of pedestrian red light running and mixed traffic characteristics in Chinese cities, with simulation results agreeing with field observations after parameter adjustment
limitations: The current model only models pedestrian decision making according to instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles, without considering scenarios where vehicles change states (accelerate, decelerate, etc.) | The calculation method of DT needs improvement for turning vehicles to make the model more practical | Questionnaire respondents may not represent all pedestrians, and self-reported answers may not reflect actual daily behavior, leading to calibration deviations | The model uses pedestrian's estimation of vehicle instantaneous speed rather than their actual prediction of vehicle future actions
notes: The paper addresses a unique problem specific to developing countries, particularly China, where pedestrian violations are prevalent (23.3% average illegal action rate, up to 66.7% in certain sections). The model may be applicable to micro-simulation of traffic systems in other developing cities with similar characteristics.

title: Investigating pedestrian suggestions for external features on fully autonomous vehicles: A virtual reality experiment
authors: Shuchisnigdha Deb; Lesley J. Strawderman; Daniel W. Carruth
research_problem: The aim of this research was to identify potential external features on a fully autonomous vehicle (FAV) and investigate which features would help pedestrians understand the intended behavior of a FAV at a crosswalk, improve their receptivity toward FAVs, and affect their crossing behavior. The core problem is that with FAVs, technology is primarily responsible for vehicle control, making interpersonal communication (eye contact, hand gestures) between drivers and pedestrians impossible.
method_summary: A virtual reality experiment using a pedestrian simulator (Unity 3D + HTC Vive headset). Thirty participants walked across a virtual crosswalk in front of a FAV. A 4x4 randomized complete block design tested four visual features (walking silhouette, 'braking' in text, flashing smile, no signal) and four audible features (horn sound, music, verbal message 'safe to cross', no signal). Data collection included simulator-collected objective measures (crossing time, waiting time, head tracking) and survey-based subjective measures (receptivity surveys, pedestrian behavior questionnaire, personal innovativeness scale).
method_family: Virtual reality experiment, randomized complete block design
tasks: Pedestrian-FAV interaction at crosswalk; Feature preference rating; Crossing decision making; Receptivity assessment before/after exposure
datasets: Custom VR pedestrian simulator (Unity 5 + HTC Vive); Pedestrian Behavior Questionnaire (PBQ) - 20 items; Pedestrian Receptivity Questionnaire for FAVs (PRQF); Personal Innovativeness Scale (4 items, adapted from Agarwal & Prasad, 1998); Simulation Sickness Questionnaire (SSQ)
metrics: Feature ratings (7-point scale); Crossing time; Waiting time before entering crosswalk; Receptivity scores (scenario-based surveys); Pedestrian behavior subscales (violation, error, lapse, aggression, positive behaviors)
claim_text: Pedestrians' receptivity toward FAVs significantly increased with the inclusion of external features
limitations: Single controlled scenario with FAV approaching from one direction and always stopping; no other traffic included in the virtual environment | Proposed features indicated safe crossing conditions only; did not consider negative interfaces or vehicle restart signals | First exposure scenario; complex traffic situations with signals, vehicle operators, positive/negative interfaces, and environmental sounds not investigated | Small sample size (n=30) limits generalizability; participants were mainly from communities surrounding Mississippi State University | Study designed for non-signalized intersection only; results may not generalize to signalized crosswalks
notes: VR validation study confirmed participants responded similarly in VR traffic environment as in real traffic (Deb, Carruth, et al., 2017); The walking silhouette is familiar to pedestrians from existing pedestrian signals, making it easy to interpret; The smile feature was confusing - participants could not determine if it meant 'thank you for not crossing' or 'please cross'; Verbal message considered beneficial for visually impaired pedestrians and in poor visibility conditions; Participants with higher personal innovativeness showed lower waiting time before crossing

metrics: Feature ratings (7-point scale); Crossing time; Waiting time before entering crosswalk; Receptivity scores (scenario-based surveys); Pedestrian behavior subscales (violation, error, lapse, aggression, positive behaviors)
claim_text: Pedestrians who often commit errors or show aggressive behaviors toward other road users rated FAV implementation poorly, even with external features

title: Investigating pedestrian suggestions for external features on fully autonomous vehicles: A virtual reality experiment
authors: Shuchisnigdha Deb; Lesley J. Strawderman; Daniel W. Carruth
research_problem: The aim of this research was to identify potential external features on a fully autonomous vehicle (FAV) and investigate which features would help pedestrians understand the intended behavior of a FAV at a crosswalk, improve their receptivity toward FAVs, and affect their crossing behavior. The core problem is that with FAVs, technology is primarily responsible for vehicle control, making interpersonal communication (eye contact, hand gestures) between drivers and pedestrians impossible.
method_summary: A virtual reality experiment using a pedestrian simulator (Unity 3D + HTC Vive headset). Thirty participants walked across a virtual crosswalk in front of a FAV. A 4x4 randomized complete block design tested four visual features (walking silhouette, 'braking' in text, flashing smile, no signal) and four audible features (horn sound, music, verbal message 'safe to cross', no signal). Data collection included simulator-collected objective measures (crossing time, waiting time, head tracking) and survey-based subjective measures (receptivity surveys, pedestrian behavior questionnaire, personal innovativeness scale).
method_family: Virtual reality experiment, randomized complete block design
tasks: Pedestrian-FAV interaction at crosswalk; Feature preference rating; Crossing decision making; Receptivity assessment before/after exposure
datasets: Custom VR pedestrian simulator (Unity 5 + HTC Vive); Pedestrian Behavior Questionnaire (PBQ) - 20 items; Pedestrian Receptivity Questionnaire for FAVs (PRQF); Personal Innovativeness Scale (4 items, adapted from Agarwal & Prasad, 1998); Simulation Sickness Questionnaire (SSQ)
metrics: Feature ratings (7-point scale); Crossing time; Waiting time before entering crosswalk; Receptivity scores (scenario-based surveys); Pedestrian behavior subscales (violation, error, lapse, aggression, positive behaviors)
claim_text: Pedestrians who intentionally violate traffic rules and those who get distracted became more cautious in presence of FAVs and appreciated the external features
limitations: Single controlled scenario with FAV approaching from one direction and always stopping; no other traffic included in the virtual environment | Proposed features indicated safe crossing conditions only; did not consider negative interfaces or vehicle restart signals | First exposure scenario; complex traffic situations with signals, vehicle operators, positive/negative interfaces, and environmental sounds not investigated | Small sample size (n=30) limits generalizability; participants were mainly from communities surrounding Mississippi State University | Study designed for non-signalized intersection only; results may not generalize to signalized crosswalks
notes: VR validation study confirmed participants responded similarly in VR traffic environment as in real traffic (Deb, Carruth, et al., 2017); The walking silhouette is familiar to pedestrians from existing pedestrian signals, making it easy to interpret; The smile feature was confusing - participants could not determine if it meant 'thank you for not crossing' or 'please cross'; Verbal message considered beneficial for visually impaired pedestrians and in poor visibility conditions; Participants with higher personal innovativeness showed lower waiting time before crossing

title: An extension of the theory of planned behavior to predict pedestrians' violating crossing behavior using structural equation modeling
authors: Hongmei Zhou; Stephanie Ballon Romero; Xiao Qin
research_problem: Examined pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB), extending the theory by adding descriptive norm, perceived risk and conformity tendency to evaluate their respective impacts on pedestrians' behavior intentions.
method_summary: Survey-based study using structural equation modeling (SEM) to analyze data from 260 respondents in Dalian, China. A questionnaire presented a scenario where pedestrians crossed the road violating pedestrian lights at an intersection. The study tested the basic TPB components (instrumental attitude, subjective norm, perceived behavioral control) and extended factors (descriptive norm, perceived risk, conformity tendency). Four models were built and compared using goodness of fit indices.
method_family: Structural equation modeling (SEM) with questionnaire survey
tasks: Predicting pedestrians' violating road-crossing behavior intentions
datasets: Survey data from 260 respondents in Dalian, China
metrics: CMIN/df ratio; RMSEA; PCLOSE; CFI; Cronbach's alpha; Regression weights
claim_text: Instrumental attitude was a significant predictor of pedestrians' violating crossing behavior intention across all four models tested
limitations: Bias of reported behavior, especially negative behavior - actual behavior should be observed for comparison or validation | Relatively small number of items in each construct to maintain internal reliability, which could affect the results | Sample limited to one city (Dalian, China), which may limit generalizability to other cultural contexts
notes: Study found that Model 4 (with instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency) provided the best predictive utility; The survey used a scenario-based questionnaire about crossing against pedestrian lights; All four models showed adequate goodness of fit, with Model 4 achieving the best fit (CFI=0.996, RMSEA=0.030); Results support using descriptive norms, conformity tendency, and instrumental attitude to predict pedestrians' violations rather than basic TPB alone

title: Effects of mobile phone distraction on pedestrians' crossing behavior and visual attention allocation at a signalized intersection: An outdoor experimental study
authors: Kang Jiang; Feiyang Ling; Zhongxiang Feng; Changxi Ma; Wesley Kumfer; Chen Shao; Kun Wang
venue: Accident Analysis and Prevention, Volume 115, Pages 170-177
research_problem: This study investigates how mobile phone distractions (music distraction, phone conversation distraction, and text distraction) affect pedestrians' crossing behavior and visual attention allocation at a signalized intersection, comparing the severity of different types of mobile phone distractions on pedestrian safety.
method_summary: Outdoor-environment experiment conducted with 28 college student pedestrians at a signalized intersection. Two HD video cameras recorded crossing behavior, and Tobii Pro Glasses 2 eye tracker recorded visual attention. Participants completed crossing trials under four conditions: undistracted (S0), music distraction (S1), phone conversation distraction (S2), and text distraction (S3). Eight dependent variables were measured: three crossing behavior indicators (initiate time, look left/right, crossing speed) and five eye movement indicators (pupil diameter, scanning frequency, fixation points, fixation time, fixation duration) across five areas of interest.
method_family: Field experiment with eye-tracking
tasks: Pedestrian street crossing under different mobile phone distraction conditions
metrics: Initiate time (time between green light and crossing initiation); Look left and right (frequency while crossing); Crossing speed (mean walking speed); Pupil diameter (mean diameter reflecting information processing effort); Scanning frequency (scans per unit time); Fixation points (count across AOIs); Fixation time (duration on each AOI); Fixation duration (mean duration on each AOI)
claim_text: Text distraction causes the greatest impairment to pedestrian crossing performance, followed by phone conversation distraction and music distraction, with text distraction significantly increasing initiate time (2.65s vs 0.69s undistracted), decreasing look left/right frequency (2.50 vs 4.46), and reducing crossing speed (1.36 m/s vs 1.46 m/s).
limitations: Small sample size (n=28) limited to college students, restricting generalizability to broader pedestrian population | Participants restricted from self-regulation and behavioral adaptation; may not reflect real-world mobile phone use patterns | Data collected at only one specific signalized intersection with very low traffic volume (<5 vehicles/minute), limiting ecological validity for complex traffic environments | Experimental design differs from daily life conditions: unfamiliar phone (Huawei P9), mental arithmetic tasks for conversation/texting may not accurately represent typical mobile phone use | Eye-tracking technology limitations: participants did not wear glasses, which may exclude populations with vision impairments who also use mobile phones
notes: Study used 40-km/h speed limited intersection with 14-meter-wide crosswalk, 21-second green light and 39-second red light; Eye tracker: Tobii Pro Glasses 2 at 50-HZ sampling rate; Five Areas of Interest (AOIs) defined: mobile phone area, crosswalk area, traffic signal area, right traffic area, left traffic area; All three distraction conditions showed statistically significant differences (p<0.05) from undistracted baseline on most measures; Supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China and Anhui Provincial Natural Science Foundation

title: Roles of personal and environmental factors in the red light running propensity of pedestrian: Case study at the urban crosswalks
authors: Dianchen Zhu; N.N. Sze; Lu Bai
research_problem: Identifying factors that affect the propensity of red light running violation of pedestrians at signalized intersections, examining both personal factors (demographics, behavior) and environmental factors (presence of other pedestrians, signal time, traffic condition)
method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong, capturing crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians during 652 signal cycles. A random parameter logit model was developed to measure associations between red light running propensity and explanatory factors, accounting for unobserved heterogeneity in pedestrian behavior.
method_family: Random parameter logit model (binary logit regression with normally distributed random parameters)
tasks: Pedestrian red light running violation analysis; Factor analysis of crossing behavior
datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed at 6 signalized crosswalks in Hong Kong during 652 signal cycles
metrics: Binary outcome (red light running vs. not running); Odds ratios; Marginal effects/elasticities; AIC for model comparison
claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males (odds ratio = 0.667)
limitations: Study focuses on factors affecting red light running propensity but does not assess safety outcomes (crash risk) | Effect of vehicular speed on pedestrian red light running behavior was not measured despite its relevance to crash risk and severity | Random parameter distribution assumption (normal) may not capture all unobserved heterogeneity; other distributions were not tested | Study limited to urban crosswalks in Hong Kong; generalizability to other contexts with different traffic signal systems (e.g., countdown displays) may be limited
notes: Study addresses gap in literature by combining individual-level (personal demographics, behavior) and cycle-level (traffic condition, signal time) factors; Novel contribution includes examining influence of social norms through presence/behavior of other pedestrians in same cycle; Hong Kong pedestrian signal system has three phases: steady green, flashing green, and steady red (no countdown display); Model 2 with interaction effects showed superior fit (lower AIC: 2750.1 vs 2776.6)

title: A model of pedestrians' intended waiting times for street crossings at signalized intersections
authors: Baibing Li
research_problem: The paper addresses the inadequate modeling of pedestrian waiting times at signalized intersections. Existing models (exponential distribution) fail to capture the bounded nature of waiting times during the red-man phase and cannot represent the observed U-shaped distribution of pedestrian behavior.
method_summary: The paper proposes a statistical model for pedestrian waiting times based on a bounded Pareto distribution extended to account for pedestrian-vehicle interaction via statistical unconditioning. Pedestrians are classified into risk-taking and risk-averse categories based on their effective critical headway (ECH) and sensitivity to excess headway. The model results in a mixture distribution with four components that can produce U-shaped, J-shaped, or L-shaped distributions depending on traffic conditions and pedestrian types.
method_family: Statistical Modeling; Mixture Models; Bounded Distribution Theory
tasks: Modeling pedestrian intended waiting times during red-man phase; Characterizing pedestrian street-crossing behavior
datasets: Field data from signalized intersection in Kunming, China (283 valid observations)
metrics: Chi-square goodness-of-fit test
claim_text: The proposed model produces a U-shaped distribution that characterizes pedestrian street-crossing behavior, showing large proportions of pedestrians who cross immediately and those willing to wait for the entire red-man phase
limitations: The model focuses on univariate analysis with only pedestrian waiting time as the explicit variable | Data collected from only one intersection in Kunming, China - generalizability to other locations may be limited | Pedestrian classification into only two categories (risk-taking and risk-averse) may oversimplify real-world behavioral diversity
notes: The model introduces the effective critical headway (ECH) concept - the minimum vehicle time headway required by a pedestrian to cross safely. The sensitivity coefficient b relates the excess headway to waiting time behavior. The EM algorithm is used for parameter estimation with BIC for model selection. Empirical findings show young pedestrians tend to be risk-takers while elderly pedestrians are more risk-averse, with 13.8% crossing immediately and 50.6% willing to wait for entire red phase.

title: Exploring factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China
authors: Weihua Zhang; Kun Wang; Lei Wang; Zhongxiang Feng; Yingjie Du
research_problem: The study investigates factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at signalized intersections in China, which is a critical contributor to pedestrian-involved traffic crashes. The research addresses the gap in understanding how individual characteristics, trip features, and pedestrian attitudes collectively influence red-light running decisions.
method_summary: A questionnaire-based SP (Stated Preference) survey was conducted in Hefei, China, collecting data on pedestrian socioeconomic characteristics, trip features, and attitudes toward crossing facilities. Using 631 valid samples from six signalized intersections, a binomial logistic regression model was developed. The Score test was used for variable selection at the 95% confidence level, followed by iterative calculation to identify significant predictors.
method_family: Binomial logistic regression
tasks: Analyze factors influencing pedestrian red-light running behavior at signalized intersections
datasets: Survey data collected from Hefei, China (May-June 2015); 631 valid samples from 6 signalized intersections
metrics: Binary outcome (run red-light vs. not run); Odds ratios; Statistical significance (p<0.05)
claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running probability: trip purpose, time period in a day, attitude towards running red-light when in hurry, and attitude towards whether road facility quality affects crossing behavior
limitations: Data collected from only one Chinese city (Hefei); results may not generalize to other cities or countries due to cultural and driving behavior differences | Study only examined signalized intersections, excluding other crossing facility types such as midblock crossings with/without buttons | Analysis focused on individual pedestrian behaviors, ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons and group dynamics | Sample size (631) may be insufficient for fully capturing regional variations within the city
notes: The study addresses a critical traffic safety issue in China where pedestrian-involved crashes account for a quarter of all traffic deaths. The binomial logistic model provides a probability prediction function (Equation 3) that can be used to identify high-risk pedestrian groups and intersections. Key policy implications include improving road crossing facilities, implementing traffic signal控制系统, and strengthening law enforcement in rush hours and areas with faster life rhythms.

title: Modeling the interaction between vehicle yielding and pedestrian crossing behavior at unsignalized midblock crosswalks
authors: Jing Zhao; Jairus Odawa Malenje; Jingxian Wu; Ruoming Ma
research_problem: The paper addresses the interaction between vehicles and pedestrians at unsignalized midblock crosswalks, where drivers and pedestrians must make simultaneous decisions about yielding and gap acceptance. While pedestrian gap acceptance, vehicle yielding behavior, and microscopic traffic flow models have been studied separately, no existing work converges these behaviors into a car-following model to analyze traffic flow effects.
method_summary: The study extends the Full Velocity Difference (FVD) car-following model by integrating probabilistic logit models for vehicle yielding (VY) and pedestrian gap acceptance (PGA) behaviors. The VY logit model considers gap size, number of lanes, number of waiting pedestrians, median location, and e-police presence. The PGA logit model considers gap size, crossing distance, waiting time, and pedestrian position. The combined model simulates vehicle-pedestrian interactions through time-step simulation in MATLAB, determining whether vehicles yield based on pedestrian crossing status and whether pedestrians accept gaps based on the logit probability.
method_family: Microscopic Traffic Flow Model / Car-Following Model / Full Velocity Difference (FVD) Model Extension
tasks: Pedestrian gap acceptance behavior modeling; Vehicle yielding behavior modeling; Vehicle-pedestrian interaction simulation; Traffic flow analysis at unsignalized midblock crosswalks; Sensitivity analysis of traffic and geometric factors
datasets: Data from 13 midblock crosswalk locations in Shanghai, China (for VY and PGA model development); Empirical data from 295 Zhenhua Rd. and 1565 Lingshi Rd. in Shanghai (for model validation)
metrics: Average vehicular delay (seconds); Average pedestrian waiting time (seconds); Relative error between simulated and surveyed values
claim_text: The proposed model successfully describes the interaction between vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance behaviors at unsignalized midblock crosswalks
limitations: The model does not consider illegal pedestrian crossing behavior, which is typical in developing countries | The study does not account for the combined effect of midblock crosswalks and adjacent intersections on traffic flow | The model uses parameters calibrated for Shanghai, China, which may not generalize to other regions with different traffic cultures
notes: The paper received funding from National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant number 71971140); Published in Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour; The VY model was developed by Malenje et al. (2019) and PGA model by Zhao et al. (2019); The LOS B threshold for vehicular delay at signalized intersections (20 seconds) from Highway Capacity Manual 2010 was used as the criteria for recommending signal control

title: Study on pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections
authors: Marisamynathan.; Vedagiri Perumal
venue: Journal of Traffic and Transportation Engineering (English Edition)
research_problem: Understanding pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections under mixed traffic conditions, including crossing speed variations, signal compliance rates, and pedestrian-vehicular interactions to identify influencing factors for improving pedestrian safety
method_summary: Data collected through videography at 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai, India. 775 pedestrian samples were observed during morning and evening peak hours. Statistical tests including ANOVA, Pearson's correlation coefficient, and Student t-test were performed. Logistic regression models were developed to model odds of pedestrian noncompliance and pedestrian-vehicular interaction.
method_family: Statistical analysis (ANOVA, Pearson correlation, t-test, logistic regression)
tasks: Analyze pedestrian crossing speed variations; Analyze pedestrian signal compliance behavior; Analyze pedestrian-vehicular interactions; Identify factors influencing crossing behaviors; Develop logistic regression models for noncompliance and interaction prediction
datasets: 775 pedestrian samples from 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai (Swami Vivekanand Junction, Mahim Junction, Holkar Junction)
metrics: Pedestrian compliance rate (percentage crossing during green phase); Crossing speed (mean, SD, 15th/50th/85th percentile); Crossing Speed Deviation Factor (CSDF); Odds Ratio (OR) from logistic regression
claim_text: Male pedestrians cross faster than female pedestrians at signalized intersections
limitations: Sample size of 775 pedestrians across 3 intersections may not fully represent all traffic conditions | Study conducted in Mumbai, India only - results may not generalize to other regions with different traffic patterns | Crossing speed and compliance behavior may vary significantly in non-peak hours
notes: Crossing Speed Deviation Factor (CSDF) was newly established as a ratio of crossing speed variation to average crossing speed; Logistic regression models showed male pedestrians have higher noncompliance rates; Adult pedestrians have higher odds (8.074) of vehicle interaction compared to children; Overall pedestrian compliance rate was only 44%, with significant variation across junctions (34%-64%)

title: A probabilistic model of pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections for connected vehicles
authors: Yoriyoshi Hashimoto; Yanlei Gu; Li-Ta Hsu; Miho Iryo-Asano; Shunsuke Kamijo
research_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of predicting pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections to prevent collisions between turning vehicles and pedestrians. The goal is to recognize pedestrian crossing decisions (cross or wait) and motion types (standing, walking, running) within seconds from traffic signal and pedestrian position information, which can be obtained via Connected Vehicle technology.
method_summary: The authors propose a Dynamic Bayesian Network (DBN) model that probabilistically integrates relationships among intersection context information (traffic signal phases) and pedestrian behavior (decisions and motion types). The model follows the flow of pedestrian behavior: assessing the situation (signal phase, position relative to crosswalk), making decisions (cross/wait), determining motion type (standing/walking/running), and executing physical movement. Particle filter is used for Bayesian filtering to estimate states including position, crossing decision, and motion type.
method_family: Probabilistic Graphical Models (Dynamic Bayesian Network) with Particle Filter inference
tasks: Pedestrian crossing decision recognition (cross vs wait); Motion type classification (standing, walking, running); Position estimation
datasets: Real traffic data collected from a vehicle at a signalized intersection in Japan - 289 pedestrian sequences (251 cross-scenarios, 38 wait-scenarios) with manually labeled pedestrian grounding points, motion types, crosswalk edge positions, and traffic signal phases
metrics: Decision recognition accuracy; Motion recognition accuracy; Positioning error (mean and standard deviation)
claim_text: The proposed DBN-based system can correctly recognize pedestrian crossing decisions with >85% accuracy after 2 seconds of observation for cross decisions and 3-4 seconds for wait decisions
limitations: The model assumes pedestrians make crossing decisions only at the onset of PFG, which may not reflect real-world behavior where pedestrians can change decisions later | Running motion is difficult to distinguish from walking due to overlapping speed distributions; recognition rate for running is only 35-56% compared to 90% for walking | The model only predicts straight trajectories, cannot handle curved pedestrian trajectories which occur in the dataset | Computation time is relatively high (5.1s to process 1.0s of data with 2000 particles), requiring optimization for real-time applications | The model does not account for additional contextual factors such as the presence and positions of turning vehicles, which affect pedestrian decisions
notes: This paper belongs to the Virtual Special Issue on: Connected Autonomous Vehicles; The study focuses on left-hand traffic context (Japan) with near-side pedestrians approaching left-turning vehicles; Pedestrian signals in Japan have three phases: PG (pedestrian green), PFG (pedestrian flashing green), and PR (pedestrian red); The model uses gamma distributions for context-dependent speed modeling and combines constant-speed with context-speed models

title: Modeling pedestrian crossing speed profiles considering speed change behavior for the safety assessment of signalized intersections
authors: Miho Iryo-Asano; W.K.M. Alhajyaseen
research_problem: Pedestrian safety at signalized intersections is compromised by unpredictable sudden speed changes (acceleration/deceleration) that pedestrians exhibit while crossing. These behavioral changes cannot be anticipated by conflicting vehicles, leading to hazardous situations. The study aims to quantitatively model these sudden speed changes to improve safety assessment at signalized crosswalks under uncongested conditions.
